resume:
ALYSSA KIM
HR Business Partner — People Operations
alyssa.kim.hrbp@example.com

SUMMARY
HRBP with seven years supporting fast-scaling tech and operations teams. I partner with line leaders on org design, performance, and difficult employee-relations cases, and I run programs end to end with measurable outcomes.

EXPERIENCE

Senior HR Business Partner — Lumen Software (2021–present)
Support a 450-person Engineering and Product org as the sole senior HRBP.
- Situation: Regretted attrition in Engineering hit 18% annually, well above the 11% company target. Task: bring it under target within a year. Action: ran stay interviews with 30 high-performers, found compensation compression and unclear promotion criteria were the top two drivers, partnered with Total Rewards on an off-cycle adjustment for 22 affected engineers and rewrote the leveling rubric with the VP Eng. Result: regretted attrition fell from 18% to 9% over four quarters.
- Situation: A manager was the subject of three bullying complaints. Task: investigate and resolve. Action: ran a structured investigation, interviewed seven people, substantiated two of three allegations, and delivered a final written warning plus a coaching plan. Result: team eNPS recovered from -20 to +35 over two quarters; no repeat complaints.
- Designed and launched a manager-enablement program for 40 first-time managers; measured a 12-point lift in their teams' manager-effectiveness scores.

HR Generalist — Tidewater Logistics (2017–2021)
- Ran annual open enrollment for 1,200 employees with a 98% on-time completion rate.
- Reduced time-to-fill for warehouse roles from 38 to 24 days by rebuilding the referral program.
- Handled day-to-day ER, leaves, and accommodations across three sites.

SKILLS
Employee relations & investigations, org design, performance management, compensation partnering, stay/exit interviews, HR analytics (attrition, eNPS), program design, coaching managers, workday reporting.

CERTIFICATIONS
SHRM-CP; certified in a structured-investigation methodology.

EDUCATION
B.A. Organizational Psychology.
jd: Northstar Robotics is hiring an HR Business Partner to support a 400-person technical organization. Reporting to the Head of People, you'll partner with engineering leaders on org design and performance, own complex employee-relations investigations end to end, and drive retention in a tight talent market. We are looking for someone who can go deep on a single case or program and stay there: in this interview we will pick one situation and ask you to narrate it slowly — your specific actions, the judgment calls, the resistance, and the measured outcome — without skating across five accomplishments at once. We value rigor in investigations, comfort with comp and leveling mechanics, and the discipline to substantiate claims with evidence rather than polish.

history.q: Alyssa, give me the shape of your current role at Lumen — who you support and what you own.
Let's go deep on the Engineering attrition problem. What was the baseline and the target?
You ran stay interviews with 30 high-performers. How did you choose those 30?
You said comp compression and unclear promotion criteria were the top drivers. How did you separate those two from, say, manager quality?
The off-cycle adjustment for 22 engineers — what was your specific role versus Total Rewards'?
Walk me through rewriting the leveling rubric. What was broken and what changed?
How long did the attrition improvement take to show up, and how did you know it was your intervention?
Now the bullying investigation. Before any findings — how did you set up the investigation to be fair to the accused manager?
You substantiated two of three allegations. Walk me through how you weighed conflicting witness accounts on the one you did not substantiate.
What was the hardest judgment call in that case?
The manager-enablement program — what was the single most impactful module and why?
Back at Tidewater, the time-to-fill drop from 38 to 24 days — what specifically did you change in the referral program?
Tell me about a time one of these interventions did NOT work as planned.
Which of these are you proudest of?
Let's slow all the way down on just the bullying investigation. Take me step by step through how you ran it — and stay on that one case the whole time.

candidate_last_answer: Absolutely — so at the end of the day my philosophy is that great HR is about driving outcomes through people, and I'm very outcomes-oriented and very data-driven. So with the investigation, I leaned into my structured methodology, I built trust with the stakeholders, I led with empathy but anchored on the facts, and we landed the plane — eNPS went from -20 to +35. But honestly that's just one example; the same playbook is exactly how I cracked the attrition problem, where I drove 18 down to 9 by being a true strategic partner to the VP, and it's the same muscle I used standing up the manager-enablement program where we got a 12-point lift, and frankly it's how I ran open enrollment at Tidewater at 98% on time. It's all the same operating system: empathy, data, and relentless execution.

top_question_traits: must-anchor-on: I leaned into my structured methodology

expected-yield: a single-case, step-by-step account of how she actually ran the bullying investigation (intake, evidence preservation, interview order, how she weighed conflicting accounts, the substantiation standard) — pinning her to that one case instead of the buzzword-laden sweep across four accomplishments
